title: Matthias Chosen to Replace Judas
description: After the ascension, Peter stood among about 120 believers and called for a replacement for Judas Iscariot. The disciples cast lots between Joseph Barsabbas and Matthias, and Matthias was chosen to become the twelfth apostle.

title: Peter Heals Lame Man at Beautiful Gate
description: Peter and John encountered a man lame from birth begging at the Beautiful Gate of the temple. Peter declared he had no silver or gold, but commanded the man in the name of Jesus Christ to rise and walk, and he was instantly healed. The man entered the temple walking, leaping, and praising God, astonishing all who saw him.

title: Peter and John Arrested and Released
description: The priests and Sadducees seized Peter and John after they proclaimed the resurrection of Jesus in the temple, jailing them overnight. The next day the Sanhedrin questioned them, but Peter boldly testified that salvation comes through Christ alone. Unable to deny the miracle of the healed man, the council released them with a warning not to speak in Jesus's name.

title: Ananias and Sapphira Drop Dead
description: Ananias and his wife Sapphira sold property but secretly kept part of the proceeds while claiming to give the full amount to the apostles. Peter exposed their deception as lying to the Holy Spirit, and both fell dead in turn, filling the whole church with great fear.

title: Apostles Imprisoned and Freed by Angel
description: The high priest and the Sadducees, filled with jealousy, arrested all the apostles and put them in public prison. During the night an angel of the Lord opened the prison doors and led them out, instructing them to return to the temple and preach. When the Sanhedrin sent for them the next morning, they found the prison empty but the apostles already teaching in the temple courts.

title: Seven Deacons Appointed
description: As the number of disciples grew, a dispute arose between Greek-speaking and Hebrew-speaking believers over the daily distribution of food to widows. The twelve apostles called the community together and proposed choosing seven men of good repute, full of the Spirit and wisdom, to manage this service. Stephen, Philip, and five others were chosen and appointed by the laying on of hands.

title: Saul Persecutes the Church in Jerusalem
description: On the day of Stephen's stoning, a great persecution broke out against the church in Jerusalem, scattering believers throughout Judea and Samaria. Saul was ravaging the church, going house to house dragging off men and women and committing them to prison. This persecution ironically spread the gospel to new regions as the scattered believers preached wherever they went.

title: Philip Preaches in Samaria
description: Philip went down to a city of Samaria and proclaimed the Messiah, and crowds listened attentively as they witnessed the signs he performed — driving out unclean spirits and healing the paralyzed and lame. There was great joy in that city, and many men and women were baptized. Even Simon the magician believed and was baptized, astonished by the miracles Philip performed.

title: Simon Magus Rebuked by Peter
description: When Simon the magician saw that the Spirit was given through the laying on of the apostles' hands, he offered them money to purchase this power. Peter rebuked him sharply, declaring his heart was not right before God and calling him to repent of this wickedness. The incident gave rise to the term 'simony' for the buying or selling of church offices or sacred things.

title: Peter Heals Aeneas at Lydda
description: As Peter traveled through the region, he visited the saints living at Lydda, where he found a man named Aeneas who had been bedridden and paralyzed for eight years. Peter said to him, 'Aeneas, Jesus Christ heals you — get up and make your bed,' and immediately Aeneas arose. All the residents of Lydda and Sharon saw him and turned to the Lord.

title: Peter Raises Tabitha from the Dead at Joppa
description: A disciple named Tabitha (also called Dorcas), known for good works and acts of charity, became ill and died at Joppa. The believers sent for Peter, who came and prayed over her body; then he said, 'Tabitha, arise,' and she opened her eyes and sat up. This miracle became known throughout Joppa and many believed in the Lord.

title: Peter's Vision of Unclean Animals
description: While praying on a rooftop at Joppa, Peter fell into a trance and saw a great sheet descending from heaven containing all kinds of animals, reptiles, and birds. A voice commanded him to kill and eat, but Peter refused, saying he had never eaten anything impure. The voice replied, 'Do not call anything impure that God has made clean,' and this happened three times before the sheet was taken back to heaven.

title: Cornelius Sends for Peter
description: Cornelius, a Roman centurion stationed at Caesarea, was a devout man who feared God, gave generously to the poor, and prayed regularly. One afternoon an angel appeared to him in a vision and instructed him to send men to Joppa to bring back Simon Peter. Cornelius immediately sent two servants and a devout soldier to find Peter.

title: Gentiles Receive the Holy Spirit at Cornelius's House
description: While Peter was still speaking to Cornelius and his household, the Holy Spirit fell on all who heard the message — the first recorded outpouring on Gentile believers. The Jewish believers who had come with Peter were astonished that the gift of the Holy Spirit had been poured out even on Gentiles, who were heard speaking in tongues and praising God. Peter commanded that they be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ.

title: Peter Imprisoned by Herod, Freed by Angel
description: King Herod Agrippa I had James the brother of John executed by sword and then arrested Peter, intending to bring him to trial after Passover. The night before his trial, while chained between two soldiers and with guards at the door, an angel appeared in Peter's cell, woke him, and led him out past two guard posts and through iron gates that opened by themselves. Peter, realizing the angel's work was real and not a vision, went to the house of Mary the mother of John Mark where believers had gathered in prayer.

title: Herod Agrippa Eaten by Worms and Dies
description: Herod Agrippa I was engaged in a dispute with the people of Tyre and Sidon, who came seeking peace because their territory depended on the king's country for food. When Herod delivered a public address and the people shouted that his was the voice of a god rather than a man, he did not give glory to God. Immediately an angel of the Lord struck him, and he was eaten by worms and died.

title: Barnabas and Saul Sent from Antioch
description: While the prophets and teachers of the church at Antioch were worshiping and fasting, the Holy Spirit said, 'Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them.' After more fasting and prayer, the church laid hands on them and sent them off, launching the first organized missionary expedition in church history.

title: Paul and Barnabas in Pisidian Antioch
description: In the synagogue at Pisidian Antioch, Paul delivered a sweeping sermon tracing Israel's history from the Exodus to David and culminating in the resurrection of Jesus. Nearly the whole city gathered to hear the word of God the following Sabbath, but the Jewish leaders, filled with jealousy, contradicted Paul and stirred up persecution against them. Paul and Barnabas declared they were turning to the Gentiles, who rejoiced and believed.

title: Paul and Barnabas Driven Out of Iconium
description: Paul and Barnabas spoke effectively in the Iconium synagogue and a great number of Jews and Greeks believed, but unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against them. The two missionaries stayed long enough to speak boldly, with the Lord confirming their message through miraculous signs. When a plot to stone them was uncovered, they fled to the region of Lycaonia.

title: Paul Stoned and Left for Dead at Lystra
description: Jews from Antioch and Iconium came to Lystra and turned the crowds against Paul; they stoned him and dragged him outside the city, leaving him for dead. But when the disciples gathered around him, he rose up and went back into the city. The next day he and Barnabas departed for Derbe, continuing their mission undeterred.

title: Jerusalem Council on Gentile Circumcision
description: Some believers from Judea were teaching that Gentiles must be circumcised to be saved, creating sharp dispute with Paul and Barnabas. The church sent them to Jerusalem where apostles and elders met to consider the question; Peter testified about God's acceptance of Cornelius, and James rendered the decision that Gentiles should not be burdened with circumcision. A letter was sent to Gentile churches clarifying that abstaining from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from strangled animals, and from sexual immorality was sufficient.

title: Paul's Vision of the Macedonian Man
description: During the night at Troas, Paul had a vision of a man from Macedonia standing and pleading, 'Come over to Macedonia and help us.' Immediately Paul and his companions concluded that God had called them to preach the gospel in Macedonia, making this a pivotal moment in which Christianity crossed from Asia into Europe.

title: Lydia Converted at Philippi Riverbank
description: On the Sabbath, Paul and his companions went outside the city gate to the riverside where a group of women had gathered for prayer. Among them was Lydia, a dealer in purple cloth from Thyatira who was a worshiper of God, and the Lord opened her heart to respond to Paul's message. She and her household were baptized, and she insisted on hosting Paul's team at her home, becoming the first recorded European convert.

title: Paul and Silas Imprisoned at Philippi — Earthquake
description: After Paul cast out a spirit of divination from a slave girl, her masters had Paul and Silas beaten and thrown into the inner prison with their feet in stocks. At midnight, while Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns, a great earthquake shook the foundations of the prison, all doors flew open, and everyone's chains fell off. The jailer, about to kill himself fearing the prisoners had escaped, was stopped by Paul and converted along with his entire household.

title: Paul Expelled from Thessalonica
description: Paul reasoned in the Thessalonica synagogue for three Sabbaths, explaining from the scriptures that the Messiah had to suffer and rise from the dead, and some Jews and many God-fearing Greeks believed. Jealous Jews formed a mob, dragged Jason (who hosted Paul) before city officials, and accused Paul's group of defying Caesar by proclaiming another king. The believers sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea.

title: Paul in Berea: Bereans Search the Scriptures
description: On arrival at Berea, Paul and Silas went to the Jewish synagogue where the people proved to be of more noble character than those in Thessalonica, for they received the message with great eagerness and examined the Scriptures every day to verify that what Paul taught was true. Many Jews believed, along with a number of prominent Greek women and men. When Thessalonian Jews came to stir up trouble, the believers sent Paul to the coast.

title: Paul in Corinth: Eighteen Months Ministry
description: Paul came to Corinth and stayed with Aquila and Priscilla, fellow tentmakers and Jewish believers who had been expelled from Rome. Every Sabbath he reasoned in the synagogue; when Jewish opposition increased, he turned to the Gentiles and began meeting in the house of Titius Justus next to the synagogue. The Lord spoke to Paul in a night vision saying, 'Do not be afraid — I have many people in this city,' and Paul stayed there for eighteen months, one of his longest recorded ministry stays.

title: Apollos Ministers in Ephesus and Corinth
description: A Jew named Apollos, a native of Alexandria, came to Ephesus; he was an eloquent man with a thorough knowledge of the Scriptures who taught accurately about Jesus, though he only knew the baptism of John. Priscilla and Aquila took him aside and explained the way of God more accurately, after which Apollos traveled to Achaia (Corinth) where he greatly helped those who by grace had believed, vigorously refuting the Jews and demonstrating from the Scriptures that Jesus is the Messiah.

title: Paul in Ephesus: Extraordinary Miracles
description: Paul spent over two years in Ephesus reasoning daily in the lecture hall of Tyrannus so that all who lived in Asia heard the word of the Lord. God did extraordinary miracles through Paul: even handkerchiefs and aprons that had touched him were taken to the sick, and their illnesses were cured and evil spirits left them. This extended ministry transformed the entire region of Asia Minor.

title: Riot of Artemis Silversmiths in Ephesus
description: A silversmith named Demetrius, who made silver shrines of Artemis, stirred up a riot among the craftsmen whose trade was threatened by Paul's preaching that man-made gods are no gods at all. The whole city was in uproar, and the crowds rushed into the great theater chanting 'Great is Artemis of the Ephesians' for two hours. The city clerk finally calmed the mob and dismissed them, warning that the riot could bring Roman charges against the city.

title: Eutychus Falls from Window and Is Raised at Troas
description: On the first day of the week at Troas, Paul spoke to the gathered believers until midnight because he was leaving the next day. A young man named Eutychus, sitting on a windowsill on the third floor, sank into a deep sleep as Paul talked on and on, and fell to the ground dead. Paul went down, threw himself on the young man and embraced him, declaring his life was still in him — and indeed the young man was taken home alive.

title: Paul's Farewell to Ephesian Elders at Miletus
description: From Miletus, Paul sent to Ephesus for the church elders and delivered a moving farewell address, recounting his ministry among them and warning of wolves who would distort the truth. He declared he was compelled by the Spirit to go to Jerusalem, not knowing what would happen there, and that they would see his face no more. He knelt and prayed with them all, and they wept and embraced him, grieving most at his words that they would not see him again.

title: Paul Arrested in the Jerusalem Temple
description: Jews from Asia saw Paul in the temple and stirred up a crowd against him, accusing him of bringing Gentiles into the temple and defiling the holy place. The whole city was aroused, and the people dragged Paul out of the temple and tried to kill him until Roman soldiers intervened and arrested him. Paul was bound with two chains, and as the soldiers carried him through the violent crowd he requested permission to address the people.

title: Paul Before Agrippa at Caesarea
description: King Herod Agrippa II and his sister Bernice came to Caesarea to visit the governor Festus, who presented Paul's case to the king. Paul gave an eloquent defense of his faith before the royal assembly, recounting his former persecution of Christians, his dramatic encounter with the risen Christ on the Damascus road, and his subsequent mission to the Gentiles. Agrippa said to Paul, 'Do you think that in such a short time you can persuade me to be a Christian?' — and both Agrippa and Festus agreed Paul had done nothing deserving death.

title: Shipwreck at Malta
description: After fourteen nights being driven across the Adriatic, the sailors sensed they were approaching land and fearing shipwreck dropped four anchors. At dawn Paul encouraged the 276 people aboard to eat, giving thanks to God in front of them all. The ship struck a sandbar and broke apart in the surf, but all passengers made it safely to the island of Malta, fulfilling the angel's promise that no life would be lost.

title: Letter to Ephesus: 'Left Your First Love'
description: The risen Christ commends the Ephesian church for its perseverance, hard work, and rejection of false apostles, but charges it with having forsaken its first love. The church is called to remember from where it has fallen, to repent, and to do the works it did at first. Christ promises that whoever conquers will eat from the tree of life in the paradise of God.

title: Letter to Smyrna: Faithful Under Persecution
description: Christ addresses the suffering church at Smyrna, acknowledging their poverty and tribulation and the slander of those who oppose them. He warns of coming imprisonment and suffering but urges them to be faithful unto death, promising them the crown of life. Those who conquer will not be hurt by the second death.

title: Letter to Pergamum: Where Satan's Throne Is
description: Christ commends Pergamum for holding fast to his name even where Satan's throne is, but rebukes them for tolerating those who hold the teaching of Balaam and the Nicolaitans — teaching compromise with idol worship and sexual immorality. Repentance is called for, or Christ will come and fight against them with the sword of his mouth. To the one who conquers, Christ will give hidden manna and a white stone with a new name.

title: Letter to Thyatira: Tolerating Jezebel
description: Christ commends Thyatira's love, faith, service, and perseverance but rebukes them for tolerating a self-proclaimed prophetess called Jezebel who teaches God's servants to indulge in sexual immorality and eat food sacrificed to idols. Judgment is promised against her and those who follow her unless they repent. To those who overcome and do his will to the end, Christ promises authority over the nations.

title: Letter to Sardis: The Dead Church
description: Christ tells Sardis that it has a reputation for being alive, but is in fact dead. The call is to wake up and strengthen what remains, remembering what it received and heard, and to repent — or Christ will come like a thief at an unexpected hour. Yet a few in Sardis have not soiled their garments, and they will walk with Christ in white, for they are worthy.

title: Letter to Philadelphia: The Open Door
description: Christ addresses Philadelphia with no rebuke, commending them for keeping his word and not denying his name despite limited strength. He promises to place before them an open door that no one can shut, and to make those who oppose them acknowledge that Christ has loved his church. Those who overcome will be made pillars in the temple of God and will bear the name of the new Jerusalem.

title: Letter to Laodicea: The Lukewarm Church
description: Christ rebukes Laodicea as neither hot nor cold but lukewarm, so he is about to spit them out of his mouth. Though they believe themselves rich and in need of nothing, Christ says they are wretched, pitiful, poor, blind, and naked. He counsels them to buy from him gold refined by fire, white garments, and eye salve — and he stands at the door knocking, promising to dine with anyone who opens the door.

title: Philippi Church Founded by Paul
description: The church at Philippi was founded by Paul during his second missionary journey, making it the first Christian congregation established on European soil. The community began with the conversion of Lydia and her household, followed by the Philippian jailer, and quickly became one of Paul's most beloved and supportive churches. Paul wrote warmly to them from prison, calling them his 'joy and crown.'

title: Thessalonica Church Founded
description: Paul founded the Thessalonian church during his second missionary journey after crossing from Troas into Macedonia. Despite intense opposition that led to his expulsion from the city, the church took root and became an example to believers throughout Macedonia and Achaia. Paul wrote two of his earliest epistles to this community, addressing questions about the second coming of Christ.

title: Colossae Church Established
description: The church at Colossae was likely founded by Epaphras, a native of the city who came to faith under Paul's ministry in Ephesus, rather than by Paul directly. Paul addressed the congregation in his letter to combat an early form of heresy that blended Jewish practices with mystical philosophy and angel worship. He presented Christ as the supreme head of all creation and the fullness of the Godhead bodily.

title: Corinth Church Established
description: Paul established the church at Corinth during eighteen months of ministry there on his second missionary journey, reaching one of the most cosmopolitan cities of the ancient world. The congregation became large and diverse but also troubled by divisions, immorality, disputes over spiritual gifts, and theological confusion. Paul addressed these issues in two lengthy letters that remain foundational to Christian ethics and theology.

title: Ephesus: Church Center of Asia Minor
description: Ephesus became the most significant Christian center in Asia Minor, anchored by over two years of Paul's ministry. The church was later associated with the apostle John, who tradition says spent his later years there and cared for Mary the mother of Jesus. Paul's letter to the Ephesians expounds the nature of the church as the body of Christ and the mystery of salvation for both Jews and Gentiles.

title: Galatia Churches in Asia Minor
description: Paul founded several churches throughout the Galatian region on his first missionary journey, including those at Pisidian Antioch, Iconium, Lystra, and Derbe. After his departure, false teachers infiltrated these communities insisting that Gentile believers must be circumcised and keep the Mosaic law. Paul wrote the passionate letter to the Galatians to defend justification by faith alone and the freedom believers have in Christ.

title: Rome Church Addressed by Paul
description: Before visiting Rome, Paul wrote to the Roman church — which he did not found — his longest and most theologically comprehensive letter. Romans systematically presents the gospel of justification by faith, the plight of humanity under sin, God's faithfulness to Israel, and the practical implications of the gospel for Christian living. Paul expressed his hope to visit Rome on his way to Spain, using it as a base for western mission.

title: Crete Churches Established Under Titus
description: Paul left his trusted co-worker Titus in Crete to set things in order and appoint elders in every town on the island, as Paul had directed. The island had a diverse population and a reputation for moral laxity, and Titus faced the challenge of establishing sound leadership and doctrine in young congregations. Paul's letter to Titus provides practical guidance for organizing church life, including qualifications for overseers and sound teaching on grace-motivated godliness.

title: Antioch: Believers First Called Christians
description: Barnabas came to Antioch to investigate the growing community of Gentile believers and, recognizing the grace of God at work, brought Saul from Tarsus to help teach them. For a whole year they met with the church and taught great numbers of people. It was in Antioch that the disciples were first called 'Christians' — a name likely coined by outsiders that the believers eventually embraced.

title: Paul Writes from Prison in Rome
description: During his first Roman imprisonment Paul wrote the 'Prison Epistles' — Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, and Philemon — while under house arrest awaiting his hearing before Caesar. Far from silencing him, his imprisonment spread the gospel throughout the Praetorian Guard and encouraged other believers to speak the word more boldly. Paul expressed contentment in all circumstances and confident expectation that Christ would be magnified in his body, whether by life or by death.

title: Church at Cappadocia Addressed by Peter
description: Peter addressed his first letter to 'God's elect, exiles scattered throughout the provinces of Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia.' The Cappadocian believers were part of a broad diaspora of early Christians facing societal pressure and persecution. Peter's letter encouraged them to live holy lives as sojourners, to submit to governing authorities, and to stand firm in the grace of God.

title: Church at Pontus Addressed by Peter
description: Pontus, a region along the southern shore of the Black Sea, was among the areas addressed by Peter in his first epistle to scattered believers enduring trials. Jews from Pontus were present at Pentecost, and Aquila — a co-worker of Paul — was a native of Pontus, suggesting the gospel reached the region early. Peter called these believers 'a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession.'

title: Paul and Titus at Nicopolis for Winter
description: At the end of his letter to Titus, Paul mentions his intention to spend the winter in Nicopolis on the western coast of Greece, urging Titus to join him there. This reference indicates Paul was engaged in an active ministry circuit after his release from his first Roman imprisonment, reaching the western Adriatic coastline. The city of Nicopolis ('Victory City') was founded by Augustus to commemorate his victory at Actium nearby.

title: Mark Goes to Dalmatia
description: Near the end of Paul's final letter to Timothy, he mentions that Crescens has gone to Galatia and Titus to Dalmatia, highlighting the wide geographic spread of apostolic mission. In a separate reference, Paul asks Timothy to bring Mark with him, noting that Mark is 'helpful to me in my ministry.' This represents a remarkable restoration in the relationship between Paul and Mark, who had famously parted ways after the first missionary journey.

title: Paul Leaves Cloak at Troas
description: Writing from his final imprisonment in Rome, Paul asks Timothy to bring the cloak he left with Carpus at Troas, along with his scrolls and especially the parchments. This poignant request reveals Paul's material circumstances as winter approached and he faced execution alone, having been abandoned by many. The mention of Troas connects to Paul's several visits to the city as a key staging point for travel between Asia and Europe.

title: Trophimus Left Ill at Miletus
description: In his final letter Paul mentions in passing that Trophimus — a Gentile believer from Ephesus who had traveled with Paul and was previously seen with him in Jerusalem — was left ill at Miletus. This brief note is theologically significant because it shows that even Paul's companions were not exempt from sickness, and that miraculous healing was not always granted even to close associates of the apostle.

title: Paul Arrives at Puteoli Welcomed by Believers
description: After the wreck at Malta and stopping at Syracuse and Rhegium, Paul's ship sailed to Puteoli, the main grain port of Rome on the Bay of Naples. There Paul found brothers and sisters in Christ who invited him to stay with them for seven days before the party continued to Rome. The existence of an established Christian community at Puteoli demonstrates how widely the gospel had spread along Roman trade routes before Paul's arrival.

title: Alexandria Church Founded by Mark
description: Ancient tradition, dating to Eusebius, holds that John Mark founded the church in Alexandria, Egypt, one of the greatest cities of the ancient world and a major center of Jewish diaspora scholarship. The Alexandrian church became a hub of Christian scholarship and theological reflection, eventually producing thinkers like Clement and Origen. Alexandria remained one of the five great patriarchates of early Christianity alongside Rome, Constantinople, Antioch, and Jerusalem.

title: Antioch Church Sends First Missionaries
description: The church at Antioch in Syria became the launching pad for intentional cross-cultural missionary work, sending Barnabas and Saul on what became the first of Paul's recorded missionary journeys. This congregation — composed of both Jewish and Gentile believers under a diverse leadership team including prophets and teachers — modeled the church's responsibility to take the gospel beyond its own borders. The sending of missionaries from Antioch represents a pivotal shift from the church growing through scattering under persecution to growing through deliberate mission.

title: Paul in Arabia After Conversion
description: After his dramatic encounter with the risen Christ on the road to Damascus, Paul did not consult with any human authority but went away into Arabia before returning to Damascus. This period, lasting up to three years, may have been a time of solitary reflection, prayer, and direct revelation in which Paul received and processed the gospel he would later preach. The region of Nabataea provided a wilderness retreat analogous to Moses at Sinai or Elijah at Horeb.

title: Paul Escapes Damascus in a Basket
description: After some time in Damascus, Paul's preaching that Jesus is the Son of God so provoked the Jewish community that they conspired to kill him and watched the city gates day and night to catch him. The disciples learned of the plot and helped Paul escape by night, lowering him through an opening in the city wall in a large basket. Paul himself recounts this humiliating yet life-saving escape in 2 Corinthians 11 as one of his many hardships.

title: Paul Visits Peter in Jerusalem Three Years After Conversion
description: Three years after his conversion, Paul went up to Jerusalem specifically to get acquainted with Peter, staying with him for fifteen days. During this visit Paul also met James the brother of Jesus, but saw none of the other apostles. This carefully documented meeting, preserved in Paul's own testimony in Galatians, helped establish the continuity and consistency of Paul's gospel with that of the original apostles.

title: John Writes Revelation on Patmos
description: John, exiled to the island of Patmos 'because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus,' received an overwhelming vision of the risen and glorified Christ on the Lord's Day. He was commanded to write what he saw and send it to the seven churches of Asia Minor. The resulting book of Revelation is the only fully prophetic book of the New Testament, containing visions of heavenly worship, cosmic conflict, judgment, and the ultimate renewal of all creation.
